reference: GATE CS — Operating System / Memory Management

map.condition: Need effective memory access time with TLB hit ratio
Need effective access time with page fault rate / hit ratio
Computing number of bits for page/frame offset in paging

map.use: \text{logical address} = p_1 \;|\; p_2 \;|\; d \quad (\text{outer, inner, offset})

map.condition: Deciding page faults — which algorithm minimises?

formulas.name: Effective Access Time with TLB
formulas.plain: T_eff = h*(T_TLB + T_M) + (1-h)*(T_TLB + 2*T_M)
formulas.whenToUse: When TLB hit ratio h is given and each page-fault-free access needs 1 or 2 memory references

terms.means: TLB hit ratio (probability that page is found in TLB)
TLB lookup time
Main memory access time

formulas.trap: On a TLB miss you still pay T_TLB plus TWO memory accesses (page table + actual data), not one

formulas.name: Effective Access Time with Page Fault Rate
formulas.plain: T_eff = H * T_M + (1-H) * T_D
formulas.whenToUse: When hit ratio H (fraction of accesses satisfied by main memory) is given with disk access time T_D

terms.means: Hit ratio — fraction of accesses found in main memory
Main memory access time
Disk (secondary memory) access time

formulas.trap: T_D is orders of magnitude larger than T_M; even a small (1-H) term dominates — solve for H when efficiency target is given

formulas.name: Efficiency Condition for Effective Access Time
formulas.plain: T_eff / T_M <= k  =>  H >= (T_D/T_M - k) / (T_D/T_M - 1)
formulas.whenToUse: When a question asks: 'what must H be so that EAT is no more than k times T_M?'

terms.means: Allowed slow-down factor relative to pure main-memory access
Required minimum hit ratio

formulas.trap: Substitute numeric values for T_D/T_M first (often 1000:1); forgetting to do so gives wrong H

formulas.name: Page Offset Bits
formulas.plain: offset bits = log2(page size in bytes)
formulas.whenToUse: Converting page size to the number of bits used for the offset in a logical/physical address

terms.means: Size of one page (must be a power of 2)

formulas.trap: Page size is always a power of 2 in GATE problems; if given in KB multiply by 1024 before taking log

formulas.name: Logical Address Split (Single-Level Paging)
formulas.plain: logical address = page_number_bits | offset_bits;  total bits = log2(virtual address space)
formulas.whenToUse: Determining page number and offset fields from logical address size and page size

terms.means: Page number field (logical address bits minus offset bits)
Displacement / offset within the page

formulas.trap: Confusing logical address space with physical address space — page number bits come from logical space, frame number bits from physical space

formulas.name: Two-Level Paging Address Split
formulas.plain: logical address = outer_page_index | inner_page_index | offset
formulas.whenToUse: Two-level (hierarchical) paging — splitting bits among outer table, inner table, and offset

terms.means: Index into the outer page table
Index into the inner page table
Byte offset within the page

formulas.trap: In a 2-level scheme two memory accesses are needed for page table (outer then inner) before the actual data access — total 3 accesses without TLB

formulas.name: Page Table Size
formulas.latex: \text{Page Table Size} = \frac{\text{Virtual Address Space}}{\text{Page Size}} \times \text{Entry Size}
formulas.plain: Page Table Size = (Virtual Address Space / Page Size) * Entry Size
formulas.whenToUse: Calculating the memory footprint of a page table given virtual space, page size, and entry width

terms.means: Total addressable virtual memory
Size of one page (power of 2)
Size of each page table entry in bytes

formulas.trap: Entry size must include the frame number bits PLUS protection/valid/dirty bits, rounded up to bytes

formulas.name: Number of Physical Frames
formulas.plain: Number of frames = Physical Memory Size / Frame Size
formulas.whenToUse: Finding how many frames the physical memory holds — determines frame number field width

terms.means: Total installed RAM
Frame size equals page size

formulas.trap: Frame number bits = log2(number of frames), NOT log2(physical memory size) directly; they are the same but write the formula clearly

formulas.name: Optimal (OPT / MIN) Page Replacement
formulas.latex: \text{Replace the page whose next use is farthest in the future}
formulas.plain: On a page fault: evict the page not needed for the longest future time
formulas.whenToUse: Theoretical lower bound on page faults — requires future knowledge; used in GATE to compare other algorithms

terms.means: Optimal algorithm — minimum possible page faults for any reference string

formulas.trap: OPT is not implementable in practice (requires future references); GATE questions often ask you to trace it manually on a given string

formulas.name: LRU Page Replacement
formulas.latex: \text{Replace the page that was least recently used (longest since last reference)}
formulas.plain: On a page fault: evict the page with the oldest last-use timestamp
formulas.whenToUse: Approximates OPT using past history; no Belady's anomaly; used in GATE tracing questions

terms.means: Least Recently Used — evicts page last accessed furthest in the past

formulas.trap: A page in constant recent use will NEVER be evicted by LRU — contrast with FIFO which can evict a heavily-used page based on arrival time

formulas.name: FIFO Page Replacement
formulas.latex: \text{Replace the page that has been in memory the longest (oldest arrival)}
formulas.plain: On a page fault: evict the page that arrived earliest (queue order)
formulas.whenToUse: Simplest replacement — suffers Belady's anomaly (more frames can cause more faults)

terms.means: First-In First-Out — treats page frames as a circular queue

formulas.trap: Belady's anomaly: increasing the number of frames with FIFO can increase page faults — unique to FIFO; OPT and LRU are stack algorithms that do NOT have this anomaly

formulas.name: Belady's Anomaly
formulas.latex: \exists\; \text{reference strings where } F(n+1) > F(n) \text{ with FIFO}
formulas.plain: For some reference strings, FIFO gives MORE page faults with MORE frames
formulas.whenToUse: Answering which algorithms suffer / do not suffer Belady's anomaly

terms.means: Number of page faults with n frames

formulas.trap: Only FIFO (and some clock variants) exhibit Belady's anomaly; LRU and OPT are stack algorithms — they NEVER exhibit it

formulas.name: Page Fault Count in a Reference String
formulas.plain: Total page faults = cold-start misses + misses due to insufficient frames
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing a reference string with a given algorithm and fixed frame count to count total faults

terms.means: First reference to each unique page is always a fault (cold miss)
Faults caused by having fewer frames than active pages

formulas.trap: First n distinct pages always cause faults regardless of algorithm; don't forget to count the cold-start faults at the beginning of a trace

formulas.name: Working Set Definition
formulas.latex: WS(t,\Delta) = \{\text{pages referenced in the interval } (t-\Delta, t]\}
formulas.plain: WS(t, delta) = set of pages referenced in the last delta time units ending at time t
formulas.whenToUse: Determining which pages are in the working set at a given time t with window size delta

terms.means: Current time (reference position in the string)
Working set window size (number of recent references to look back)

formulas.trap: The window is the LAST delta references, not future ones; pages referenced outside the window are no longer in the working set even if they will be used soon

formulas.name: Working Set Size Over Time
formulas.plain: |WS(t,delta)| <= min(delta, total unique pages in program)
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding the number of frames a process needs at any instant to avoid thrashing

terms.means: Number of distinct pages in working set at time t

formulas.trap: Working set size changes over time; allocate frames equal to max working set size to prevent thrashing

formulas.name: Internal Fragmentation (Fixed Partitions / Paging)
formulas.plain: Internal Fragmentation = allocated block size - actual process size
formulas.whenToUse: Fixed-partition or paging systems where memory is allocated in fixed-size units

terms.means: Size of the fixed partition or page frame allocated
Actual memory required by the process

formulas.trap: Paging eliminates external fragmentation but introduces internal fragmentation in the last page; worst case is (page size - 1) bytes wasted

formulas.name: External Fragmentation (Variable Partitions)
formulas.latex: \text{External Frag: total free memory} \geq \text{request, but no single hole is large enough}
formulas.plain: External Fragmentation: enough total free memory exists but it is split into non-contiguous holes
formulas.whenToUse: Variable-partition / contiguous allocation when compaction is not done

terms.means: A contiguous free region of memory

formulas.trap: The smallest request that CANNOT be satisfied equals (largest hole + 1) bytes when no compaction is done

formulas.name: Allocation Fit Strategies
formulas.latex: \text{First-Fit: first hole} \geq \text{size} \quad \text{Best-Fit: smallest hole} \geq \text{size} \quad \text{Worst-Fit: largest hole}
formulas.plain: First-Fit: first adequate hole | Best-Fit: tightest fit | Worst-Fit: largest hole
formulas.whenToUse: Choosing or comparing allocation strategies in variable-partition memory management problems

terms.means: Fastest search; leaves large holes intact at the end
Minimises wasted space per allocation; creates many tiny unusable holes
Maximises leftover hole size; often performs worst overall

formulas.trap: Best-Fit does NOT always give the least overall fragmentation in practice; First-Fit often performs better overall

formulas.name: TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) Role
formulas.plain: Physical Address = Frame_number * Page_Size + offset  (frame found via TLB on a hit)
formulas.whenToUse: Explaining address translation when a TLB hit occurs — avoids memory access for page table

terms.means: Page number from logical address
Offset from logical address
Frame number returned by TLB for page p

formulas.trap: TLB is a content-addressable (associative) memory — lookup is parallel, not sequential; on a miss, page table in main memory must be consulted (extra memory access)

formulas.name: Memory Accesses per Reference (TLB scenario)
formulas.latex: \text{Accesses} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{TLB hit (only data)} \\ 2 & \text{TLB miss, single-level paging} \\ k+1 & \text{TLB miss, k-level paging} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Memory accesses = 1 (TLB hit) | 2 (miss, 1-level) | k+1 (miss, k-level paging)
formulas.whenToUse: Counting actual memory bus accesses when TLB is present

terms.means: Number of page table levels

formulas.trap: TLB lookup itself is not a memory access (it is a hardware cache); only RAM accesses count in EAT formulas
